---
title: "Partial Observability, Belief States, and RL/Control Bridges"
description: "How hidden state turns sequential decision-making into belief-state planning, why POMDPs extend MDPs, and where control and RL meet in partially observed settings."
page-type: concept
status: published
update-status: current
level: advanced
topic: stochastic-control-and-dynamic-programming
prerequisites:
  - /topics/advanced/stochastic-control-and-dynamic-programming/concepts/value-iteration-policy-iteration-and-approximate-dynamic-programming.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/control-and-dynamics/concepts/estimation-kalman-filtering-and-the-separation-principle.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/probability/index.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/statistics/index.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Explain why partial observability replaces state-based planning with belief-based planning."
  - "Describe a POMDP at a first-pass level through hidden state, observation, action, and belief update."
  - "Interpret the belief state as a sufficient information state for decision-making."
  - "Recognize where classical control, approximate planning, and reinforcement learning meet in partially observed problems."
applications:
  - control-and-dynamics
  - engineering-systems
  - machine-learning-theory
keywords:
  - partial observability
  - belief state
  - POMDP
  - filtering
  - reinforcement learning
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-25
source-check-date: 2026-04-25
review-cadence: annual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
related-pages:
  - /topics/advanced/stochastic-control-and-dynamic-programming/index.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/stochastic-control-and-dynamic-programming/concepts/stochastic-linear-systems-lqg-and-the-separation-principle.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/stochastic-control-and-dynamic-programming/concepts/continuous-time-stochastic-control-and-hamilton-jacobi-bellman-intuition.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/control-and-dynamics/concepts/estimation-kalman-filtering-and-the-separation-principle.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/control-and-dynamics/concepts/learning-based-control-system-identification-and-rl-bridges.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/probability/index.qmd
stable-sources:
  - "Stanford AA228 Decision Making Under Uncertainty"
  - "Stanford SISL Decision Making Under Uncertainty text"
  - "Stanford POMDP slides"
current-sources:
  - "Stanford AA228 course page"
  - "Stanford AA228 solutions state uncertainty"
  - "Stanford SISL decision making under uncertainty text"
  - "Stanford POMDP slides"
  - "Stanford MS&E 235A course page"
---

## Role

This is the seventh page of the `Stochastic Control and Dynamic Programming` module.

Its job is to explain what changes when the controller does not directly observe the state.

That is the missing final step in the first-pass spine:

- first we planned with known states
- then we handled stochastic dynamics
- now we handle hidden state and noisy observations

This is where `MDP` reasoning becomes `POMDP` reasoning.

## First-Pass Promise

Read this page after [Stochastic Linear Systems, LQG, and the Separation Principle](stochastic-linear-systems-lqg-and-the-separation-principle.qmd).

If you already read [Continuous-Time Stochastic Control and Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman Intuition](continuous-time-stochastic-control-and-hamilton-jacobi-bellman-intuition.qmd), that helps, but it is not a hard prerequisite for the hidden-state story.

If you stop here, you should still understand:

- why partial observability changes the decision problem
- what a belief state is
- why belief states restore a Markov-style planning viewpoint
- where control and RL meet in partially observed systems

## Why It Matters

Many decision systems do not reveal the true state directly.

We may only see:

- noisy sensor readings
- delayed outputs
- partial measurements
- proxies rather than the latent variable we care about

So choosing actions from the raw observation alone can be fundamentally insufficient.

The system may look ambiguous:

- the same observation can correspond to different hidden states
- different hidden states may require different actions

The right object is then not the hidden state itself, because we do not know it.

It is the belief about the hidden state.

That belief becomes the new information state for planning.

## Prerequisite Recall

- in an MDP, the current state is enough to summarize all relevant past information
- Kalman filtering already showed one structured example where noisy observations are turned into a state estimate
- stochastic control introduced value functions and Bellman-style planning under uncertainty
- probability and statistics matter because beliefs are conditional distributions updated by evidence

## Intuition

### Observation Is Not State

In a partially observed problem, the controller receives an observation `o_t`, not the hidden state `s_t`.

So the immediate question is:

`what should the agent remember from the past in order to act well now?`

The answer is not usually the last observation by itself.

### The Belief State Summarizes What We Know

A belief state is the conditional distribution of the hidden state given everything observed so far.

At first pass, read it as:

`the current posterior over the hidden state`

So instead of planning in the original state space, we plan in a space of beliefs.

### Belief Dynamics Restore A Markov Story

This is the key structural idea.

Although the hidden state is not observed, the belief state evolves by a recursive update using:

- previous belief
- chosen action
- new observation

That means the belief state itself is Markov.

So the partially observed problem can be recast as a fully observed decision problem on belief space.

### This Is Where Control Meets RL Most Directly

In classical control, the bridge is:

- estimate hidden state
- act using that estimate

In RL and planning, the bridge is:

- maintain a belief or latent state
- optimize future behavior from that information state

Both are trying to solve the same problem:

`act well when the true state is not directly available`

## Formal Core

::: {#def-pomdp}
## Definition: POMDP

A partially observed Markov decision process includes:

- a hidden state `S_t`
- an action `A_t`
- a state transition law
- an observation `O_t`
- an observation law conditioned on the hidden state
- a reward or cost rule
:::

The hidden state drives the system, but the decision-maker only sees observations.

::: {#def-belief-state}
## Definition: Belief State

The belief state `b_t` is the conditional distribution of `S_t` given the action-observation history up to time `t`.
:::

At first pass, it is enough to remember:

`belief = posterior over hidden state`

::: {#thm-belief-update}
## Theorem Idea: Belief Update

Given the previous belief, the chosen action, and the new observation, the next belief is determined by a Bayesian filtering update.
:::

So the belief evolves recursively, just like a state estimate.

::: {#thm-belief-mdp}
## Theorem Idea: Belief-State Reduction

The partially observed problem can be rewritten as a fully observed control problem on the belief state.
:::

That is the structural reason dynamic programming still applies.

::: {#thm-belief-bellman}
## Theorem Idea: Bellman Equation On Belief Space

If we treat the belief `b` as the state, then the optimal value function satisfies a Bellman equation over beliefs:

$$
V(b)=\min_a \left\{ c(b,a) + \mathbb{E}[V(b') \mid b,a] \right\}
$$

or the reward-maximizing analog.
:::

At first pass, the point is not the exact formula.

It is that Bellman reasoning survives, but now on a harder state space: the space of distributions.

## Worked Example

Imagine a robot moving in a corridor with two hidden locations:

- `Left`
- `Right`

It can:

- move
- observe a noisy beacon

Suppose the beacon reading is imperfect:

- the same reading may occur in both locations
- but with different probabilities

If the robot only uses the latest reading, it may oscillate or make inconsistent choices.

If instead it keeps a belief

$$
b_t(\text{Left}),\quad b_t(\text{Right}),
$$

then each action and observation update that belief.

Planning can now use:

- immediate action cost
- expected future cost under the updated belief

So the robot is not planning over the true hidden location directly.

It is planning over its current uncertainty about location.

That is the first-pass heart of POMDP reasoning.

## Computation Lens

When you meet a partially observed decision problem, ask:

1. what is hidden?
2. what is actually observed?
3. what belief or filtered estimate summarizes the useful past?
4. is the method doing exact belief updates, approximate filtering, or learned latent-state tracking?
5. is the control or RL method planning in the original state space, the belief space, or a learned surrogate?

Those questions usually reveal whether a paper is doing classical POMDP reasoning, structured filtering and control, or modern approximate RL.

## Application Lens

### Robotics And Navigation

Robots rarely know their exact state. They infer it from noisy sensors and must act under uncertainty about position, velocity, or map structure.

### Operations And Hidden Regimes

Queueing, diagnosis, and maintenance problems often involve latent system modes that are only indirectly observed.

### RL And Sequential Inference

Many RL problems are effectively partially observed, so memory, filtering, latent-state estimation, and belief tracking become central rather than optional.

## Stop Here For First Pass

If you stop here, retain these five ideas:

- partial observability means the controller does not directly know the true state
- the right replacement object is the belief state
- the belief state is a posterior distribution updated from actions and observations
- belief states restore a Markov planning viewpoint
- this is a core meeting point of filtering, control, planning, and RL

## Go Deeper

The strongest adjacent live pages are:

- [Stochastic Control and Dynamic Programming](../index.qmd)
- [Stochastic Linear Systems, LQG, and the Separation Principle](stochastic-linear-systems-lqg-and-the-separation-principle.qmd)
- [Estimation, Kalman Filtering, and the Separation Principle](../../control-and-dynamics/concepts/estimation-kalman-filtering-and-the-separation-principle.qmd)
- [Learning-Based Control, System Identification, and RL Bridges](../../control-and-dynamics/concepts/learning-based-control-system-identification-and-rl-bridges.qmd)
- [Probability](../../../foundation/probability/index.qmd)

The strongest downstream bridges are:

- [Applications > Machine Learning](../../../../applications/machine-learning/index.qmd)
- [Information Theory](../../information-theory/index.qmd)

## Optional Deeper Reading After First Pass

- [Stanford AA228 / CS238](https://aa228.stanford.edu/) - official current course page for decision making under uncertainty with MDP, POMDP, and RL-facing framing. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [AA228/CS238 solutions: State Uncertainty](https://aa228.stanford.edu/solutions/) - official course material with direct explanations of POMDPs, belief states, and belief updates. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Decision Making Under Uncertainty text](https://web.stanford.edu/group/sisl/public/dmu.pdf) - official Stanford-hosted text with explicit sections on POMDPs, belief-state MDPs, and belief updating. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [POMDP slides](https://web.stanford.edu/~mykel/pomdps.pdf) - official Stanford-hosted slides focused directly on POMDPs and belief states. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford MS&E 235A / EE 283: Markov Decision Processes](https://web.stanford.edu/class/msande235a/index.html) - official current course page for the broader MDP and dynamic-programming arc. Checked `2026-04-25`.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [Stanford AA228 / CS238](https://aa228.stanford.edu/) - `First pass` - official current decision-making-under-uncertainty course page with POMDP and RL-facing framing. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [AA228/CS238 solutions: State Uncertainty](https://aa228.stanford.edu/solutions/) - `First pass` - official course material with direct belief-state and belief-update explanations. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Decision Making Under Uncertainty text](https://web.stanford.edu/group/sisl/public/dmu.pdf) - `First pass` - official Stanford-hosted text with explicit POMDP and belief-state sections. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [POMDP slides](https://web.stanford.edu/~mykel/pomdps.pdf) - `First pass` - official Stanford-hosted slides focused directly on partial observability and belief states. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford MS&E 235A / EE 283: Markov Decision Processes](https://web.stanford.edu/class/msande235a/index.html) - `Second pass` - official current course page for the broader MDP and dynamic-programming context. Checked `2026-04-25`.
